html:
AQA GCSE English Literature — Power and Conflict Anthology — 10 Most Likely Comparison Questions to Come Up (2026)

AQA English Literature Paper 2 — Section B (Anthology Poetry) Q26. 30 marks. Recommended writing time: 45 minutes. AOs: AO1 (response + textual reference) / AO2 (writer's methods) / AO3 (context). AQA prints the FULL TEXT of ONE named anchor poem on the paper; the student then selects ONE OTHER poem from the anthology to compare against it from memory. The stem opens "Compare how poets present [theme] in '[named anchor]' and in one other poem from 'Power and conflict'." — for example, the real 2023 stem was "Compare how poets present the ways people are affected by conflict in 'Kamikaze' and in one other poem from 'Power and conflict'." AQA names the anchor; the second poem is the student's choice.

How this list was generated

This Q-bank was built using Sophicly's prediction model adapted for the Power and Conflict cluster's comparative-question shape (see `Power-and-Conflict-AQA-Prediction-Algorithm.md` in this folder). The algorithm scores each of the 15 anthology poems on six signals — frequency as anchor in past papers, cycle gap since last anchored, coverage gap (poems never anchored in the 2017-2025 window), unexplored-angle signal (unexplored stem-themes for that poem), core-topic boost (the six spine poems AQA returns to most), and an exact-stem-repeat penalty. The cluster has set nine of fifteen poems as anchor across 2017-2025 with no repeats; six poems remain never-set within window and are therefore strong candidates for 2026 even when they are not on the spine. The same priority-order pattern from the per-text Q-banks carries over here: spine poems (Ozymandias, London, My Last Duchess, Charge of the Light Brigade, Exposure, Kamikaze) get core-topic boost despite earlier-window appearances, because AQA cannot avoid the cluster's moral core forever.

Section IDs (Q1–Q10) ARE the the model priority order. This differs from the per-text Q-banks, where the section IDs are stable references and a separate priority table reorders them. Because the P&C anchor-poem ranking is the document's spine, Q1 = highest-confidence anchor, Q10 = lowest within the top 10.

Priority order — rehearse in this order (anchor predictions)

This table ranks the 10 most likely anchor poems (the poem AQA prints in full on the paper) for 2026. The second column lists Sophicly's recommended comparison candidate — the poem these plans rehearse — but in the exam the student is free to choose any other anthology poem that fits the stem's theme.

| the model rank | Predicted anchor poem (AQA names) | Confidence | Likely 2026 stem theme | Sophicly's suggested comparison poem (student choice) |

|---:|---|---|---|---|

| 1 | Charge of the Light Brigade (Tennyson, 1854) | HIGH | Heroism / commemoration in conflict | Bayonet Charge (Hughes) |

| 2 | Ozymandias (Shelley, 1818) | HIGH | Power and time / the futility of human ambition | My Last Duchess (Browning) |

| 3 | My Last Duchess (Browning, 1842) | HIGH | Power and control over others | Ozymandias (Shelley) |

| 4 | Exposure (Owen, 1917-18) | HIGH | Power of nature in conflict | Storm on the Island (Heaney) |

| 5 | Kamikaze (Garland, 2013) | MED-HIGH | Memory across generations | Poppies (Weir) |

| 6 | Poppies (Weir, 2009) | MED | Grief and loss in conflict | Kamikaze (Garland) |

| 7 | War Photographer (Duffy, 1985) | MED | Suffering and indifference | Remains (Armitage) |

| 8 | The Emigrée (Rumens, 1993) | MED | Identity and displacement | Checking Out Me History (Agard) |

| 9 | The Prelude (Wordsworth, 1799-1850) | MED | Power of nature / the individual mind | Storm on the Island (Heaney) |

| 10 | Checking Out Me History (Agard, 2005) | MED | Identity and the power of history | The Emigrée (Rumens) |

Coverage Pack (rehearse but not top-10):

• London (Blake, 1794) — set 2024 at power/place; recency-penalised.

• Tissue (Dharker, 2006) — set 2022 at power; gap rebuilding.

• Remains (Armitage, 2008) — set 2023 at memory/guilt; gap rebuilding.

• Storm on the Island (Heaney, 1966) — set 2019 at power of nature; rotation rules out a return at the same angle.

• Bayonet Charge (Hughes, 1957) — set 2025; recency-penalised.

How to use this document

The AQA question shape. AQA Paper 2 Section B Q26 prints the full text of ONE named anchor poem on the paper. The stem then asks the student to "Compare how poets present [theme] in '[named anchor]' and in one other poem from 'Power and conflict'." The student selects the comparison poem from memory. There is no comparison poem printed — the second poem is the student's free choice from the remaining fourteen.

How this Q-bank works. Each of the ten Qs below predicts a different anchor poem AQA may print in 2026 and rehearses a plan against ONE recommended comparison candidate, plus 2–3 defensible alternatives. Every BP weaves BOTH poems together using AQA's mandated INTEGRATED-COMPARISON shape — BP1 compares FORM, BP2 compares STRUCTURE, BP3 compares LANGUAGE — each BP delivering comparative TTECEA+C so that no sentence is pure-Poem-A or pure-Poem-B. If a student walks into the exam and the printed anchor matches one of the ten predictions but they prefer a different comparison from the alternatives or anywhere else in the anthology, the same plan adapts — the comparison poem swaps in across BP1, BP2 and BP3, keeping the FORM / STRUCTURE / LANGUAGE three-paragraph architecture intact.

Rehearsal priority. Sections Q1–Q10 are already ordered by the model's confidence in the anchor prediction (Q1 = highest, Q10 = lowest within the top 10). If exam time is tight, rehearse the top 5 first (Q1–Q5). For comprehensive coverage, rehearse all 10. Memorise the cross-question reuse table at the end so the same prepared pair-foundations cover multiple Qs.

Substitution rule. In the exam, if a student prefers a comparison poem the plan does not rehearse — say Tissue against Ozymandias instead of My Last Duchess — the plan's anchor-poem content stays valid across BP1, BP2 and BP3; only the comparison-poem side swaps in fresh material. The seven-sentence comparative TTECEA+C shape, the FORM / STRUCTURE / LANGUAGE three-paragraph architecture, and the AO weighting do not change.

Pedagogical depth — three non-negotiables for HIGH-confidence picks

Every Grade-9 essay on a P&C comparison should thread at least one of these moves into one BP or the conclusion. They are the cluster's deepest analytical pivots; Sophia teaches them once so students can deploy them across any Q.

1. Name WHICH power, then ask WHO PAYS — the anthology's deepest second reading.

The standard reading of Power and Conflict sees the anthology as a comparison set on power-of-humans, power-of-nature, and power-of-conflict. The cluster examines power of humans over humans (My Last Duchess, London), power of the state (Charge of the Light Brigade, Kamikaze), power of nature (The Prelude, Storm on the Island, Exposure), power of memory (Remains, The Emigrée, Poppies), power of art and language (Ozymandias, Checking Out Me History), and power of time (Ozymandias, Tissue). Every comparison essay should name in its thesis WHICH power the two poems are about and HOW that power works differently in each.

The sharper second reading exposes the anthology's deepest editorial argument: the spine poems are NOT primarily about power — they are about WHAT POWER DESTROYS, and every comparison should ask WHO PAYS. Ozymandias's pride is destroyed by time (the "colossal Wreck" in the "lone and level sands" names the destruction of power as the poem's main idea, not power as display). The Duchess is destroyed by Duke Ferrara's honour code (her smile "too easily made glad" names the cost the speaker has extracted). The Light Brigade soldiers are destroyed by incompetent command (the mourning refrain "the six hundred" counts the dead, not the heroic). The Trenches' soldiers are destroyed by Pity-of-War indifference (Owen's "But nothing happens" names the cost of waiting). The anthology argues that power's main output is DESTRUCTION, and every comparison should ask WHO PAYS for the power the poem portrays. Generic "power" answers receive a Band 4 ceiling. Top-band answers specify the power, name the destruction, and identify the victim the power produces. Thread this into: Q1 Charge of the Light Brigade / Q2 Ozymandias / Q3 My Last Duchess / Q4 Exposure / Q5 Kamikaze / Q9 The Prelude / any BP3 that names the comparison's structural argument.

2. The chronological span dramatises how power changes over time — from Romantic pride-of-kings to contemporary state-violence-on-individuals.

The cluster's chronological arc is itself an argument. Romantic poems (Ozymandias, London, The Prelude, Extract from The Prelude) portray power as pride of kings or institutional pride to be criticised from the outside; the speaker is the Romantic walker, the dispossessed observer, the figure standing outside inherited authority. Victorian poems (My Last Duchess, Charge of the Light Brigade) portray power as personal control or state command, the form (dramatic monologue, ballad) carrying the moral position. War poems (Exposure, Bayonet Charge, Remains) shift the frame from power-as-political-fact to power-as-trauma-on-individuals — the soldier-speaker registers the cost the command's decision produced. Contemporary poems (Kamikaze, Poppies, Tissue, War Photographer) extend the trauma-frame to families, photographers, witnesses, and the materials power leaves behind. The chronological span thus argues that British poetry's relationship to power has moved from outside criticism (Romantic), through inside drama (Victorian), into traumatised recording (twentieth-century), and finally into ethical witnessing (contemporary). The anthology's teaching move is to dramatise this development through fifteen poems organised so the comparison forces the student to register the historical shift. Thread this into: any BP3 that pairs a Romantic poem with a contemporary poem (e.g. Ozymandias / Tissue, London / Checking Out Me History, The Prelude / War Photographer); any conclusion that names the anthology's editorial argument.

3. The comparison must trace the EFFECT DIFFERENCE — same theme, different methods, different moral knowledge.

AQA's mark scheme rewards comparison of methods and of effects produced. The strongest essays argue that the two poems share the same theme but produce different effects through different methods — the difference is the analytical event. Charge of the Light Brigade and Bayonet Charge both portray cavalry charges, but Tennyson's ballad commemorates while Hughes's anti-epic strips away the heroic story. The reader leaves Tennyson recruited and Hughes disillusioned. Naming this difference converts a parallel-description essay into a comparison essay. Without it, the essay catalogues techniques in two poems and gestures at similarity. With it, the essay argues that comparison produces moral knowledge neither poem alone can give. The difference is what carries the essay across the Band 5 / Band 6 line.

Why this depth matters pedagogically. AQA's Band 6 descriptor asks for "a convincing, critical analysis and exploration" of method, sustained across the comparison. A reading that names WHICH power, asks WHO PAYS, traces the historical shift across the cluster's chronological span, and tracks the EFFECT difference is the kind of thinking Examiner Reports name as the top-band signature. Without it, a comparison essay is parallel description. With it, the essay is critical analysis.

A universal method for finding the second reading. Every text in the AQA specification rewards the same move: identify the standard reading; then identify a second reading the same evidence supports; then read the two together. For Power and Conflict the paired readings are power-as-display/power-as-destruction (Ozymandias, MLD, CLB), Romantic-outside-criticism/contemporary-inside-trauma (the cluster's chronological shift), parallel-description/effect-difference (Q26 structural test). Sophia should generate the second reading whenever helping a student plan — the standard reading is what most students will write, and the second reading is what carries them past the band-9 line.

The seven-sentence body paragraph shape — comparative TTECEA+C for AQA integrated comparison

BP1 compares FORM, BP2 compares STRUCTURE, BP3 compares LANGUAGE. Within each BP, every sentence weaves BOTH poems together — no sentence is pure Poem-A or pure Poem-B. The seven sentences deliver comparative TTECEA+C:

1. Comparative topic sentence (AO1) — concept only, "While [Poet A] [concept A], [Poet B] [concept B]" or "Where [Poet A]…, [Poet B]…", setting up the idea-level contrast upfront with no techniques named. ~30 words max.

2. Comparative technique + evidence (AO2 + AO1) — name the FORM-related / STRUCTURE-related / LANGUAGE-related technique in each poem AND quote both poems inside the same sentence, then explain what the pair of techniques achieves.

3. Comparative close analysis (AO2) — zoom into specific words, sounds, or images in each quote, weaving back and forth (Poet A's verb against Poet B's verb; Poet A's sound-pattern against Poet B's sound-pattern).

4. Comparative effects (AO2) — what each poem makes the reader experience, compared. Use precise emotion words (pity, dread, exhaustion, complicity, awe, bewilderment, grief, vertigo, indignation, tenderness, exposure, ironic awe, horrified complicity, claustrophobic dread).

5. Comparative author's purpose (AO1 + AO2) — hedged ("may have written", "can be read as", "a defensible reading is", "perhaps") — why each poet chose that form / structure / language move.

6. Comparative context (AO3) — one period-specific fact for each poet, compared — the context that drove each poet's specific choice.

7. Synthesising hinge — a single sentence that pivots to the next paragraph (Thus / Whereas / By contrast / However) and sums up what THIS element (form / structure / language) revealed.

Every sentence cap ~40 words. Topic sentence ~30 words max. Every claim anchored to a quote from one or both poems. Concrete connectors only (through / from / leading to / which produces) — no arrows.

Memorise these once

The Power and Conflict anthology argues that power is exercised through violence, language, institutions, and memory — and that conflict, whether between nations or between generations, takes a human cost the powerful rarely see and the powerless cannot escape. Across fifteen poems written between 1794 and 2013, the cluster traces this human cost from the Romantic criticism of tyranny (Shelley, Blake, Wordsworth), through the Victorian study of personal and state power (Browning, Tennyson), into the war poets' refusal of the heroic story (Owen, Hughes, Armitage, Weir, Duffy), and finally into the contemporary questions of displacement, identity, and post-colonial reclamation (Dharker, Rumens, Agard, Garland). Two main tensions run through the anthology: SCALE (who has power and who pays for it) and TIME (whether power systems eventually crumble, or last within the lifetime of those they damage). Every Grade-9 comparison should hold both.

Key terms (memorise once — useful across multiple Qs):

• Dramatic monologue — verse spoken by a constructed persona whose worldview the form exposes (My Last Duchess; partially Kamikaze, War Photographer).

• Pararhyme — Owen's invention; two end-words share consonant sounds but not vowels ("knive us / nervous"); produces a feeling of sonic wrongness.

• In medias res — Latin for "into the middle of things"; opening mid-action with no scene-setting (Bayonet Charge, My Last Duchess).

• Pathetic fallacy — giving natural events human emotions (Exposure's "melancholy army"; The Prelude's "huge peak, black and huge").

• Praeteritio — Latin term for saying something by saying you will not say it (the Duke's signature: "how shall I say?").

• Dactylic metre — one stressed syllable followed by two unstressed (DUM-da-da: "Half a league, half a league"); produces a galloping rhythm.

• Anaphora — repeating the same opening phrase across clauses (London's "In every"; Remains's "his bloody life in my bloody hands" repeated).

• Cyclical structure — the final image returns to the opening (War Photographer's plane-window departure to the next assignment).

• Pity of War — Owen's idea: "the poetry is in the pity"; poetry's task is the truth propaganda denies.

Cluster-specific framing (memorise once — threads into every Power and Conflict BP):

• Romantic criticism of authority — Shelley, Blake, and Wordsworth wrote during a fifty-year period in which thrones had been toppled and inherited authority stripped of legitimacy by the French Revolution. Their poems share a refusal to accept that any inherited structure (monarchy, church, grand monuments) is permanent or innocent.

• Pity of War — Owen and the WWI poets refused the inherited heroic tone. Tennyson commemorates the Light Brigade in 1854; Owen exposes the trenches in 1918. The same nation, two generations apart, reaching opposite positions on the same activity.

• Honour-code violence — My Last Duchess and Kamikaze, though separated by a century and half the world, both examine how an honour code requires violence to keep itself going. Browning's Renaissance Italy and Garland's wartime Japan reach the same diagnosis.

• Post-colonial reclamation — Agard, Rumens, and Dharker write from the inside of the displacement and erasure colonial systems produced. Their poems share a refusal to accept that identity must be controlled by the institutions that suppressed it.

---

Q1 — Charge of the Light Brigade (predicted anchor) [HIGH confidence — never set as anchor in 9-year window]

Extract

Anchor poem (printed on the AQA paper):

The Charge of the Light Brigade — Alfred, Lord Tennyson

I

Half a league, half a league,

Half a league onward,

All in the valley of Death

Rode the six hundred.

"Forward, the Light Brigade!

Charge for the guns!" he said.

Into the valley of Death

Rode the six hundred.

II

"Forward, the Light Brigade!"

Was there a man dismayed?

Not though the soldier knew

Someone had blundered.

Theirs not to make reply,

Theirs not to reason why,

Theirs but to do and die.

Into the valley of Death

Rode the six hundred.

III

Cannon to right of them,

Cannon to left of them,

Cannon in front of them

Volleyed and thundered;

Stormed at with shot and shell,

Boldly they rode and well,

Into the jaws of Death,

Into the mouth of hell

Rode the six hundred.

IV

Flashed all their sabres bare,

Flashed as they turned in air

Sabring the gunners there,

Charging an army, while

All the world wondered.

Plunged in the battery-smoke

Right through the line they broke;

Cossack and Russian

Reeled from the sabre stroke

Shattered and sundered.

Then they rode back, but not

Not the six hundred.

V

Cannon to right of them,

Cannon to left of them,

Cannon behind them

Volleyed and thundered;

Stormed at with shot and shell,

While horse and hero fell.

They that had fought so well

Came through the jaws of Death,

Back from the mouth of hell,

All that was left of them,

Left of six hundred.

VI

When can their glory fade?

O the wild charge they made!

All the world wondered.

Honour the charge they made!

Honour the Light Brigade,

Noble six hundred!

Predicted anchor (AQA-named): The Charge of the Light Brigade by Alfred, Lord Tennyson.

Suggested comparison poem (student choice — pick any other Power and Conflict poem): Bayonet Charge by Ted Hughes. Why: the same cavalry-charge subject from opposite sides of 1918 stages the Pity-of-War shift directly, so the methods-and-effects differential almost writes itself in BP3.

Alternative comparison candidates: Exposure (Owen — pararhyme registering trench reality the heroic register refuses); Remains (Armitage — post-conflict trauma against Tennyson's commemorative closure); Kamikaze (Garland — state-ideology heroism re-examined from the family side).

Question

Compare how poets present heroism in 'The Charge of the Light Brigade' and in one other poem from 'Power and conflict'. [30 marks]

Frame

Tennyson's 1854 ballad and Hughes's 1957 anti-epic (a poem that deliberately works against the heroic tradition) both portray cavalry charges separated by exactly a century, but they reach opposite moral positions on heroism. Tennyson builds commemoration as a public duty the reader should accept. Hughes strips away every heroic idea patriotic writing relies on. The reader leaves Tennyson recruited and Hughes disillusioned. The plan traces three layers of difference — form (ballad versus anti-epic free verse), structure (a poem that builds to commemoration versus one that freezes a single moment), and language (biblical, elevated wording versus plain, stripped-back wording).

Pedagogical depth to thread. The Pity of War shift. Between 1854 and 1918, Britain's public tone about combat reversed. Tennyson writes inside a heroic tradition. Hughes inherits Owen's refusal of that tradition through his father's WWI silence. The comparison captures the moment heroic writing about war becomes impossible to defend. Land this in BP3 or the conclusion.

Technique gloss:

• Ballad form (Tennyson) — a narrative poem designed to be easy to remember and recite; ballads carry stories the public should not forget.

• Dactylic metre (Tennyson) — one stressed syllable followed by two unstressed (DUM-da-da), producing a galloping rhythm.

• Refrain (Tennyson) — "the six hundred" repeats across stanzas like a tolling bell.

• In medias res (Hughes) — Latin for "into the middle of things"; opening mid-action: "Suddenly he awoke and was running".

• Cosmic imagery (Hughes) — "cold clockwork of the stars and the nations" places the soldier alone against the universe.

• Anti-epic structure (Hughes) — three stanzas that freeze one moment of psychological terror and refuse to end with a clean resolution.

• Hook (historical fact tied to thesis): On the morning of 25 October 1854, over 270 British cavalrymen were killed, wounded, or captured at Balaclava in twenty minutes after a botched order sent them charging Russian artillery. Tennyson, as Poet Laureate, was asked to square that slaughter with national honour. Hughes, writing a century later, inherited his father's permanent silence about the Western Front.

• Building sentence (anthology context + comparison axis named): Tennyson writes inside the Victorian heroic tradition that the First World War's trenches would eventually break. Hughes writes after that break, his anti-epic the inheritance of Owen's Pity of War — the idea that poetry's task is the truth propaganda denies, not the comfort it sells. Both poems depict cavalry charges, but their deepest difference is whether heroic writing about war is still honestly available to the poet after 1918.

• Thesis: Across form (ballad versus anti-epic free verse), structure (a poem that builds to commemoration versus one that freezes a single moment), and language (biblical, elevated wording versus stripped-back wording), Tennyson builds heroism as a public duty the reader inherits. Hughes strips away every heroic idea patriotic writing relies on. The reader leaves the first poem recruited and the second disillusioned.

• Comparative topic sentence (AO1): While Tennyson uses the inherited ballad to recruit the reader into public mourning, Hughes uses anti-epic free verse to strip away every heroic idea patriotic writing relies on.

• Comparative technique + evidence (AO2 + AO1): Tennyson's dactylic-trochaic ballad meter — "Half a league, half a league, / Half a league onward" — produces a galloping pulse the listener's body must follow. Hughes's in-medias-res free verse — "Suddenly he awoke and was running" — drops the reader inside a single soldier's terror with no communal frame to hold onto.

• Comparative close analysis (AO2): Tennyson's triple-stressed feet draw the reader's body into the charge before they can pause to think, while Hughes's adverb "Suddenly" and the participle "running" refuse any sense of when or where we are. The ballad's rhythm builds heroic anticipation. The free-verse fragment refuses any anticipation a heroic style could provide.

• Comparative effects (AO2): Thus Tennyson's reader experiences joining in national mourning, while Hughes's reader experiences disorienting exposure to one soldier's terror. The ballad recruits the listener into the charge. The anti-epic strips the reader of every patriotic word the inherited form had supplied.

• Comparative author's purpose (AO1 + AO3): Tennyson may have chosen the ballad because ballads are designed to be remembered and recited by whole communities — the form turning disaster into a story passed down. Hughes can be read as choosing the anti-epic because the inherited heroic form had become impossible to defend after 1918; refusing the form is itself the moral position.

• Comparative context (AO3): Tennyson wrote in 1854 as Poet Laureate, responding to William Howard Russell's Times despatch from Balaclava under public pressure to reconcile catastrophic military failure with national honour. Hughes wrote in 1957 inside the Pity-of-War tradition Owen had bequeathed, his father's permanent silence about the Western Front the personal source of his refusal.

• Synthesising hinge: Thus form itself carries the moral position each poet takes — Tennyson's ballad converts slaughter into commemoration the listener inherits, while Hughes's anti-epic registers slaughter as terror no inherited style can absorb.

• Comparative topic sentence (AO1): Where Tennyson structures the poem as a narrative that completes in commemoration, Hughes freezes one moment of psychological terror and refuses the ending the heroic story relies on.

• Comparative technique + evidence (AO2 + AO1): Tennyson's six-stanza chronological progression closes with the command-refrain "Honour the charge they made! / Honour the Light Brigade", converting the story into a public instruction. Hughes's three-stanza, interrupted progression closes with "a running man who could not stop", leaving the soldier permanently suspended mid-action.

• Comparative close analysis (AO2): Tennyson's command "Honour" repeats across the closing stanza to turn reading into participation, while Hughes's modal "could not" leaves the soldier stuck inside a present-tense terror that any ending would erase. One form completes, the other refuses to complete.

• Comparative effects (AO2): Therefore Tennyson's reader experiences narrative resolution through ritual closure, while Hughes's reader experiences narrative collapse. The refrain "the six hundred" tolls like a bell across Tennyson's stanzas. The question "In what cold clockwork of the stars and the nations / Was he the hand pointing that second?" halts Hughes's narrative mid-action with no answer the form will supply.

• Comparative author's purpose (AO1 + AO3): Tennyson may have structured the poem to make catastrophic failure bearable by converting it into a ritual the public could repeat. Hughes can be read as structuring the poem to expose patriotic words at the moment combat dissolves them — the dropped words "King, honour, human dignity, etcetera" fall away precisely because the structure refuses to recover them.

• Comparative context (AO3): Tennyson wrote inside Victorian honour culture for a newspaper-reading public that demanded a public resolution. Hughes wrote inside the post-WWI generation that had inherited Owen's "poetry is in the pity" — the same cavalry-charge subject handled by opposite kinds of structural responsibility.

• Synthesising hinge: Thus structure determines what the form can complete — Tennyson's chronological arc delivers a commemoration the listener can carry away, while Hughes's freeze-frame refuses any completion the heroic story could survive.

• Comparative topic sentence (AO1): While Tennyson's biblical, elevated wording turns slaughter into a kind of apocalyptic spectacle, Hughes's stripped, everyday wording reduces combat to animal instinct that patriotic language cannot fit around.

• Comparative technique + evidence (AO2 + AO1): Tennyson uses biblical allusion — "Into the jaws of Death, / Into the mouth of hell / Rode the six hundred" — turning the cavalry into damned souls. Hughes uses an animal simile — "a yellow hare that rolled like a flame / And crawled in a threshing circle" — turning men into terrified prey.

• Comparative close analysis (AO2): Tennyson's metaphors "jaws" and "mouth" personify Death and Hell as devouring forces that lift the soldiers' fate into myth. Hughes's adjective "yellow" (the colour of cowardice and fear) and the verb "crawled" (animal-low, undignified) strip away every dignifying word. Biblical language makes the soldiers sacred; animal language exposes them.

• Comparative effects (AO2): Therefore Tennyson's reader experiences awe at heroic damnation while Hughes's reader experiences pity at human terror. The elevated wording recruits the reader into national myth, while the stripped wording brings the reader to realise that combat reduces patriotic words themselves to "luxuries" the soldier throws aside mid-charge.

• Comparative author's purpose (AO1 + AO3): Tennyson may have used biblical wording to convert military disaster into a spiritual story the Victorian public could absorb. Hughes can be read as using animal wording to refuse every elevation patriotic writing required — the Latin-rooted dismissal "etcetera" is itself an act of verbal refusal Tennyson's form would never allow.

• Comparative context (AO3): Tennyson wrote inside a Victorian Britain in which biblical language was still the public's main vocabulary for serious public events. Hughes wrote inside a post-1918 Britain that had heard Owen's "My friend, you would not tell with such high zest / To children ardent for some desperate glory / The old Lie" — the same charge handled through opposite vocabularies.

• Synthesising hinge: Thus language itself carries the heroic position — Tennyson's biblical, elevated vocabulary recruits the reader into commemoration, while Hughes's stripped animal-and-mechanical wording strips away every grand word patriotic language had been built to sustain.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Tennyson and Hughes both write the cavalry charge, but the century between them — and Owen's refusal of heroic language in between — produces opposite moral positions. Tennyson builds heroism as a public duty the reader inherits. Hughes strips away every patriotic word the inherited style relied on.

• Controlling concept: Heroism in this anthology is not a fixed idea but a vocabulary whose availability to the poet depends on what the poet's nation can afford to remember. Victorian Britain could afford Tennyson's elevated style. Post-1918 Britain could not.

• Authors' purposes contrasted: Tennyson may have written to reconcile catastrophic military failure with national honour for a public demanding an account. Hughes can be read as writing to make the silence his father lived inside finally speakable — for a son who inherited the war as broken fragments.

• Ultimate moral about what comparing them reveals: Comparing the two reveals that truthful writing about combat shifts with the historical moment of the witness — and that poetry's deepest responsibility is not to commemorate the dead but to refuse the comfort that would let the reader forget how they died.

Q2 — Ozymandias (predicted anchor) [HIGH confidence — spine poem + 9-year gap from 2017]

Extract

Anchor poem (printed on the AQA paper):

Ozymandias — Percy Bysshe Shelley

I met a traveller from an antique land,

Who said—"Two vast and trunkless legs of stone

Stand in the desert. . . . Near them, on the sand,

Half sunk a shattered visage lies, whose frown,

And wrinkled lip, and sneer of cold command,

Tell that its sculptor well those passions read

Which yet survive, stamped on these lifeless things,

The hand that mocked them, and the heart that fed;

And on the pedestal, these words appear:

My name is Ozymandias, King of Kings;

Look on my Works, ye Mighty, and despair!

Nothing beside remains. Round the decay

Of that colossal Wreck, boundless and bare

The lone and level sands stretch far away."

Predicted anchor (AQA-named): Ozymandias by Percy Bysshe Shelley.

Suggested comparison poem (student choice — pick any other Power and Conflict poem): My Last Duchess by Robert Browning. Why: both anatomise absolute power but reach opposite time-verdicts (dismantled by desert versus preserved across centuries), which gives BP3 the cluster's deepest time-axis differential.

Alternative comparison candidates: Tissue (Dharker — power-of-time differential and the materials power leaves behind); London (Blake — Romantic critique of inherited authority, paired Romantic-poems argument); Charge of the Light Brigade (Tennyson — monumental commemoration crumbling against military commemoration commissioned).

Question

Compare how poets present ideas about power in 'Ozymandias' and in one other poem from 'Power and conflict'. [30 marks]

Frame

Shelley's 1818 sonnet and Browning's 1842 dramatic monologue both examine absolute power, but they reach opposite positions on the anthology's deepest question. Shelley's tyrant has been completely torn down by time. Browning's tyrant still holds the curtain string. The plan traces the time-axis difference — Romantic faith that monument-building power crumbles, set against Victorian recognition that aristocratic power can stay untouchable within the lifetime of those it damages.

Pedagogical depth to thread. Name WHICH power. Shelley's poem is about the power of grand ambition tested against time. Browning's is about the power of family name over a single woman's life. Both are absolute powers, but they work on different scales and produce different outcomes. The comparison captures the anthology's central tension between scale and time.

Technique gloss:

• Broken sonnet (Shelley) — a fourteen-line form that deliberately breaks both the Petrarchan and Shakespearean rhyme schemes; the break itself is the argument.

• Triple-framed narrative (Shelley) — the poet hears it from a traveller, the traveller from the desert, the desert from the sculptor.

• Dramatic monologue (Browning) — a poem spoken by an invented character whose worldview the form exposes.

• Praeteritio (Browning) — Latin term for saying something by claiming you will not say it ("how shall I say?").

• Heroic couplets (Browning) — rhymed iambic pentameter (pairs of ten-syllable rhymed lines), the sound of aristocratic polish.

• Caesura (Browning) — a mid-line pause that enacts the act of refusal ("I choose / Never to stoop").

• Hook (historical fact tied to thesis): In the winter of 1817-18, a seven-ton granite torso of Ramesses II was being unloaded at the British Museum after a sixteen-month haul from Thebes. Shelley, in a sonnet-writing contest with his friend Horace Smith, never saw it. He used the idea of it to demolish the very thing the museum had been built to celebrate: the idea that grand monuments can last forever.

• Building sentence (anthology context + comparison axis named): Browning wrote twenty-four years later in a Victorian Britain whose Industrial Revolution was already passing laws about chartered streets while leaving aristocratic power untouched. He drew on the historical Alfonso II of Ferrara to build a tyrant who — unlike Shelley's pharaoh — was not torn down by time. Instead, this tyrant preserved his authority across centuries through the dramatic monologue's careful, deliberate speech to a marriage-broker's envoy.

• Thesis: Across form (broken sonnet versus dramatic monologue), structure (triple-framed dismantling versus single sustained speech), and language (Romantic refusal of the royal command versus Victorian study of praeteritio), Shelley argues that grand monumental power is completely erased by time, while Browning argues that aristocratic power can stay untouchable within the lifetime of those it damages. The reader feels awe at one tyrant's collapse and dread at the other's continued reign.

• Comparative topic sentence (AO1): While Shelley uses a broken sonnet to enact the collapse of grand power, Browning uses a dramatic monologue to preserve aristocratic power across the centuries Shelley's pharaoh did not survive.

• Comparative technique + evidence (AO2 + AO1): Shelley's broken sonnet — neither Petrarchan nor Shakespearean, half-rhymed "stone / frown" — opens with "Two vast and trunkless legs of stone / Stand in the desert". Browning's dramatic monologue uses iambic pentameter heroic couplets and opens "That's my last Duchess painted on the wall, / Looking as if she were alive".

• Comparative close analysis (AO2): Shelley's half-rhyme "stone / frown" refuses the satisfying close of the sonnet tradition, mirroring the pharaoh's broken legacy. Browning's perfect rhyme "wall / call" seals every couplet shut, mirroring the Duke's smooth self-presentation. The broken form refuses to immortalise; the polished form refuses to crack.

• Comparative effects (AO2): Thus Shelley's reader experiences ironic awe at the gap between the intended grandeur and the form's broken delivery, while Browning's reader experiences horrified complicity at the Duke's polished speech. The sonnet that should have praised becomes the poem that tears down. The monologue that should have exposed becomes the speech that controls.

• Comparative author's purpose (AO1 + AO3): Shelley may have chosen the sonnet because it was the literary form most associated with permanence, deliberately shattering it to argue that no structure built to make power last forever can outlast time. Browning can be read as choosing the dramatic monologue because the form lets the Duke explain his terms to the envoy carefully, without ever stooping to a confession.

• Comparative context (AO3): Shelley wrote in 1818 as a Romantic radical at the end of a fifty-year revolutionary period in which thrones had been toppled and Egyptian antiquities reduced to museum curiosities. Browning wrote in 1842, drawing on Alfonso II of Ferrara — a Renaissance honour culture in which a wife's smile counted as offence serious enough for execution — for Victorian readers growing more sceptical of inherited authority but unable to dislodge it.

• Synthesising hinge: Thus the choice of form is the poet's first position on the tyrant's fate — Shelley's broken sonnet enacts the dismantling time delivers, while Browning's dramatic monologue enacts the preservation the form's polish guarantees.

• Comparative topic sentence (AO1): Where Shelley's triple-framed narrative removes the pharaoh from any present in which his commands could still work, Browning's single sustained monologue keeps the Duke's voice unbroken across every line of the poem.

• Comparative technique + evidence (AO2 + AO1): Shelley layers three narrative frames — "I met a traveller from an antique land / Who said—" opens the poem as a quotation of a quotation before "My name is Ozymandias, king of kings" even arrives. Browning's monologue is seamless, cut only by enjambment — "since none puts by / The curtain I have drawn for you, but I" — and allows no narrative interruption.

• Comparative close analysis (AO2): Shelley's volta (the turn in a sonnet) "Nothing beside remains" arrives in three monosyllables that close the empire absolutely. Browning's structural climax "I gave commands; / Then all smiles stopped together" buries a likely murder in casual enjambment that refuses any pause. One turn delivers time's final position, the other refuses to let any position close.

• Comparative effects (AO2): Therefore Shelley's reader experiences sublime release at the desert's final word on the pharaoh, while Browning's reader experiences claustrophobic entrapment inside a tyrant whose speech the structure will not interrupt. Multiplying the frames demolishes; a single voice preserves.

• Comparative author's purpose (AO1 + AO3): Shelley may have structured the poem so that each narrative layer reduces the pharaoh's authority — the Romantic poet placed as the final narrator, granting art lasting authority over political power. Browning can be read as structuring the speech as an instruction the envoy will report rather than a confession the law could prosecute.

• Comparative context (AO3): Shelley wrote inside the Romantic period, which positioned the poet as a visionary with more lasting authority than political rulers. Browning wrote inside a Victorian Britain in which aristocratic structures had survived the revolutionary century intact — the same theme handled through opposite structural choices.

• Synthesising hinge: Thus structure determines whether time can reach the tyrant — Shelley's frames let time tear down Ozymandias across three removes, while Browning's single voice closes every gap through which time might enter.

• Comparative topic sentence (AO1): While Shelley's commanding, biblical wording is denied by the desert's single-syllable emptiness, Browning's praeteritio and possessive pronouns turn violence into the polite language of aristocratic refinement.

• Comparative technique + evidence (AO2 + AO1): Shelley uses the imperative (command) inside biblical allusion — "My name is Ozymandias, king of kings; / Look on my Works, ye Mighty, and despair!" — set against the sibilant (hissing-s) closing "The lone and level sands stretch far away". Browning uses praeteritio (saying it by saying you will not say it) inside caesura — "I choose / Never to stoop" — and the possessive "My last Duchess" opens the poem on a woman reduced to property.

• Comparative close analysis (AO2): Shelley's command "Look" survives, even though it has lost the audience it was shouting at, while the alliteration "lone and level" delivers the hissing whisper of sand over stone. Browning's monosyllables "choose / Never to stoop" register the willed pause of aristocratic refinement — what Rutgers critic William Dowling calls the most menacing words in English poetry, because they carry willingness to kill rather than yield.

• Comparative effects (AO2): Therefore Shelley's reader experiences ironic awe at the gap between an imperial command and nature's indifference, while Browning's reader experiences horrified complicity as politeness becomes a tool of violence. The desert refuses the command. The praeteritio confirms exactly what the speaker did not need to say.

• Comparative author's purpose (AO1 + AO3): Shelley may have used the elevated command to demonstrate that no political vocabulary outlasts the time it tries to defeat. Browning can be read as using the language of art-appreciation — "That piece a wonder, now: Frà Pandolf's hands" — to convert murder into matter-of-fact aristocratic talk.

• Comparative context (AO3): Shelley wrote inside Romantic atheism, which withdrew the divine title "king of kings" from any earthly ruler. Browning wrote inside Victorian Britain's preserved honour culture, in which a wife's failure to value a "nine-hundred-years-old name" above all else counted as offence — opposite political vocabularies for opposite political moments.

• Synthesising hinge: Thus language itself carries whether the tyrant's words can be denied — Shelley's biblical command is dismantled by the desert's hissing indifference, while Browning's praeteritio is sealed by the very refinement it uses.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Shelley and Browning both examine absolute power, but the way time treats their tyrants produces opposite positions. Shelley's pharaoh has been completely torn down by the desert. Browning's Duke still holds the curtain string across the entire dramatic monologue, with time itself unable to reach him.

• Controlling concept: Power in this anthology has many forms — political, monumental, aristocratic, family-line — and the question of whether any given power system crumbles within the lifetime of those it damages is the cluster's central tension between scale and time.

• Authors' purposes contrasted: Shelley may have written from the Romantic radical's confidence that revolutions tear down thrones. Browning can be read as writing from the Victorian recognition that revolutions can leave aristocratic power untouched — the same political century reaching opposite conclusions about which powers history erases.

• Ultimate moral about what comparing them reveals: Comparing the two poems reveals that the anthology's most useful question is not whether power exists, but how long it lasts and at whose expense. The difference between broken sonnet and polished couplets is itself the argument that some powers are built to crumble and others to last.

My Last Duchess — Robert Browning

Ferrara

That's my last Duchess painted on the wall,

Looking as if she were alive. I call

That piece a wonder, now: Frà Pandolf's hands

Worked busily a day, and there she stands.

Will't please you sit and look at her? I said

"Frà Pandolf" by design, for never read

Strangers like you that pictured countenance,

The depth and passion of its earnest glance,

But to myself they turned (since none puts by

The curtain I have drawn for you, but I)

And seemed as they would ask me, if they durst,

How such a glance came there; so, not the first

Are you to turn and ask thus. Sir, 'twas not

Her husband's presence only, called that spot

Of joy into the Duchess' cheek: perhaps

Frà Pandolf chanced to say, "Her mantle laps

Over my lady's wrist too much," or "Paint

Must never hope to reproduce the faint

Half-flush that dies along her throat." Such stuff

Was courtesy, she thought, and cause enough

For calling up that spot of joy. She had

A heart—how shall I say?— too soon made glad,

Too easily impressed; she liked whate'er

She looked on, and her looks went everywhere.

Sir, 'twas all one! My favour at her breast,

The dropping of the daylight in the West,

The bough of cherries some officious fool

Broke in the orchard for her, the white mule

She rode with round the terrace—all and each

Would draw from her alike the approving speech,

Or blush, at least. She thanked men—good! but thanked

Somehow—I know not how—as if she ranked

My gift of a nine-hundred-years-old name

With anybody's gift. Who'd stoop to blame

This sort of trifling? Even had you skill

In speech—which I have not—to make your will

Quite clear to such an one, and say, "Just this

Or that in you disgusts me; here you miss,

Or there exceed the mark"—and if she let

Herself be lessoned so, nor plainly set

Her wits to yours, forsooth, and made excuse,

—E'en then would be some stooping; and I choose

Never to stoop. Oh, sir, she smiled, no doubt,

Whene'er I passed her; but who passed without

Much the same smile? This grew; I gave commands;

Then all smiles stopped together. There she stands

As if alive. Will't please you rise? We'll meet

The company below, then. I repeat,

The Count your master's known munificence

Is ample warrant that no just pretence

Of mine for dowry will be disallowed;

Though his fair daughter's self, as I avowed

At starting, is my object. Nay, we'll go

Together down, sir. Notice Neptune, though,

Taming a sea-horse, thought a rarity,

Which Claus of Innsbruck cast in bronze for me!

Exposure — Wilfred Owen

Our brains ache, in the merciless iced east winds that knive us . . .

Wearied we keep awake because the night is silent . . .

Low drooping flares confuse our memory of the salient . . .

Worried by silence, sentries whisper, curious, nervous,

But nothing happens.

Watching, we hear the mad gusts tugging on the wire,

Like twitching agonies of men among its brambles.

Northward, incessantly, the flickering gunnery rumbles,

Far off, like a dull rumour of some other war.

What are we doing here?

The poignant misery of dawn begins to grow . . .

We only know war lasts, rain soaks, and clouds sag stormy.

Dawn massing in the east her melancholy army

Attacks once more in ranks on shivering ranks of grey,

But nothing happens.

Sudden successive flights of bullets streak the silence.

Less deadly than the air that shudders black with snow,

With sidelong flowing flakes that flock, pause, and renew,

We watch them wandering up and down the wind's nonchalance,

But nothing happens.

Pale flakes with fingering stealth come feeling for our faces—

We cringe in holes, back on forgotten dreams, and stare, snow-dazed,

Deep into grassier ditches. So we drowse, sun-dozed,

Littered with blossoms trickling where the blackbird fusses.

—Is it that we are dying?

Slowly our ghosts drag home: glimpsing the sunk fires, glozed

With crusted dark-red jewels; crickets jingle there;

For hours the innocent mice rejoice: the house is theirs;

Shutters and doors, all closed: on us the doors are closed,—

We turn back to our dying.

Since we believe not otherwise can kind fires burn;

Nor ever suns smile true on child, or field, or fruit.

For God's invincible spring our love is made afraid;

Therefore, not loath, we lie out here; therefore were born,

For love of God seems dying.

To-night, this frost will fasten on this mud and us,

Shrivelling many hands, and puckering foreheads crisp.

The burying-party, picks and shovels in shaking grasp,

Pause over half-known faces. All their eyes are ice,

But nothing happens.

• Pararhyme (Owen) — Owen's invention; consonant rhyme without vowel rhyme ("knive us / nervous"); produces a feeling of sonic wrongness.

• Refrain (Owen) — "But nothing happens" repeats across stanzas.

• Pathetic fallacy (Owen) — giving natural events human emotions: "Dawn massing in the east her melancholy army".

• Free verse (Heaney) — a single-stanza nineteen-line refusal of inherited convention.

• Military metaphor for weather (Heaney) — "strafes invisibly," "salvo," "bombarded".

• Oxymoron (Heaney) — two contradicting words joined together: "a huge nothing".

Q8 — The Emigrée (predicted anchor) [MED confidence — never set as anchor in 9-year window]

Extract

Anchor poem (printed on the AQA paper):

The Emigrée — Carol Rumens

There once was a country... I left it as a child

but my memory of it is sunlight-clear

for it seems I never saw it in that November

which, I am told, comes to the mildest city.

The worst news I receive of it cannot break

my original view, the bright, filled paperweight.

It may be at war, it may be sick with tyrants,

but I am branded by an impression of sunlight.

The white streets of that city, the graceful slopes

glow even clearer as time rolls its tanks

and the frontiers rise between us, close like waves.

That child's vocabulary I carried here

like a hollow doll, opens and spills a grammar.

Soon I shall have every coloured molecule of it.

It may by now be a lie, banned by the state

but I can't get it off my tongue. It tastes of sunlight.

I have no passport, there's no way back at all

but my city comes to me in its own white plane.

It lies down in front of me, docile as paper;

I comb its hair and love its shining eyes.

My city takes me dancing through the city

of walls. They accuse me of absence, they circle me.

They accuse me of being dark in their free city.

My city hides behind me. They mutter death,

and my shadow falls as evidence of sunlight.

Question

Compare how poets present ideas about identity and displacement in 'The Emigrée' and in one other poem from 'Power and conflict'. [30 marks]

Frame

Rumens's 1993 free-verse meditation and Agard's 2005 Caribbean-creole counter-narrative both portray identity under institutional pressure, but their methods differ. Rumens preserves identity through private memory. Agard reclaims it through public naming. The plan traces the difference between preserving and reclaiming.

Pedagogical depth to thread. Both poems write from the inside of displacement and post-colonial erasure. The comparison captures two opposite responses to having identity suppressed: the exile's private record, set against the reclaimer's public counter-lesson.

Technique gloss:

• Fairy-tale opening (Rumens) — "There once was a country" softening politics.

• Compound adjective (Rumens) — "sunlight-clear" intensifying memory under pressure.

• Personification (Rumens) — "my city takes me dancing".

• Caribbean creole (Agard) — refusal of Standard English literary tradition as political act.

• Anaphora (Agard) — "Dem tell me / Dem tell me".

• Alternating typography (Agard) — italic for colonial curriculum, roman for Caribbean counter-narrative.

• Sculptural metaphor (Agard) — "I carving out me identity" — selfhood actively constructed.

• Hook (historical fact tied to thesis): In 1835, Thomas Macaulay submitted his Minute on Education to the British Parliament, designing the colonial curriculum that would educate generations of Caribbean children to know English history while systematically erasing their own. Agard would later challenge that curriculum directly in his 2005 poem, written in the Caribbean creole the Minute had been designed to suppress.

• Building sentence (anthology context + comparison axis named): Rumens, writing twelve years earlier in 1993 from the Cold-War era when millions of homelands had become unreachable memories overnight, used a Mediterranean-suggesting unnamed city to register a different kind of response to displacement — the exile's private record of "sunlight-clear" memory preserved against political erasure. Both poets portray identity under institutional pressure, but their methods stand at opposite ends of the private-public scale.

• Thesis: Across form (free verse with fairy-tale opening versus Caribbean-creole alternating typography), structure (three-stanza escalation from threat to defiance versus an alternating back-and-forth between colonial fact and Caribbean counter-fact), and language (passive verbs of marking and the verb "smuggle" versus active present-continuous verbs and a metaphor of carving), Rumens preserves identity through private memory under political pressure, while Agard reclaims identity through public counter-naming. Both poets together refuse the institutional authority that decides what belonging means.

• Comparative topic sentence (AO1): While Rumens uses Standard-English free verse with a fairy-tale opening to register identity preserved through private memory, Agard uses Caribbean-creole alternating typography to register identity reclaimed through public counter-naming.

• Comparative technique + evidence (AO2 + AO1): Rumens's three eight-line stanzas open with the fairy-tale convention — "There once was a country... I left it as a child / but my memory of it is sunlight-clear". Agard's Caribbean-creole free verse uses alternating italic-and-roman typography and opens on anaphora (the same opening phrase repeating) — "Dem tell me / Dem tell me / Wha dem want to tell me".

• Comparative close analysis (AO2): Rumens's fairy-tale opening softens the politics under which the violent verbs do their work, the Standard-English style letting the exile's voice pass undetected. Agard's creole "Dem" and "checking out" refuses Standard English as itself an inheritance of colonial authority — a style that fits in, set against a style that refuses to fit in.

• Comparative effects (AO2): Thus Rumens's reader experiences courageous intimacy — going with the speaker into hostile territory — while Agard's reader witnesses liberation in real time as the visual shifts between typefaces enact the "checking out" the title names.

• Comparative author's purpose (AO1 + AO3): Rumens may have chosen the unnamed city and fairy-tale opening to let the poem stand for any exile anywhere. Agard can be read as choosing Caribbean creole because every community's words tie people to place — the creole itself part of the identity being reclaimed, the form refusing the coloniser's poetic inheritance.

• Comparative context (AO3): Rumens wrote in 1993 from the Cold-War era when millions of homelands had become unreachable memories overnight. Agard wrote in 2005 from post-colonial Caribbean diaspora, born in Guyana in 1949 and moved to Britain in 1977, inside the legacy of Macaulay's 1835 Minute on Education — opposite kinds of displacement.

• Synthesising hinge: Thus the choice of form carries whether response is private or public — Rumens's Standard-English free verse preserves identity inwardly, Agard's creole alternating typography reclaims identity outwardly.

• Comparative topic sentence (AO1): Where Rumens structures the poem as a three-stanza escalation from idealised memory through threat to defiant assertion, Agard structures it as an alternating back-and-forth between colonial fact and Caribbean counter-fact, staged through shifts in typography.

• Comparative technique + evidence (AO2 + AO1): Rumens's structural escalation moves from "my memory of it is sunlight-clear" through "I have no passport, there's no way back" to "They mutter death, / and my shadow falls as evidence of sunlight". Agard's alternating refrain — "Dem tell me bout 1066 and all dat... But Toussaint L'Ouverture / no dem never tell me bout dat" — stages the back-and-forth visibly through italic-Caribbean and roman-colonial typography.

• Comparative close analysis (AO2): Rumens's structural pivot from "sunlight-clear" through "branded by an impression of sunlight" to "my shadow falls as evidence of sunlight" intensifies the metaphor as persecution mounts. Agard's structural pivot "But" — one syllable doing the entire move from passive reception to active reclamation — turns the speaker from an object of colonial story-telling into the maker of his own image.

• Comparative effects (AO2): Therefore Rumens's reader goes with the exile into hostile territory and finds the emotional landscape grows brighter under pressure, while Agard's reader witnesses liberation in real time as the typographic shifts enact the back-and-forth visibly — internal escalation, set against visible back-and-forth.

• Comparative author's purpose (AO1 + AO3): Rumens may have structured the escalation so the homeland's light intensifies as persecution mounts — making preservation defiant. Agard can be read as structuring the back-and-forth to make the colonial curriculum's selective inclusion visible on the page, the typography itself the political act.

• Comparative context (AO3): Rumens wrote inside the Cold-War-era political exile that had turned millions of homelands into memories overnight. Agard wrote inside the legacy of the colonial curriculum that taught generations to know English history while erasing Caribbean and African contributions — opposite structural choices.

• Synthesising hinge: Thus structure determines whether identity is preserved inwardly or reclaimed outwardly — Rumens's escalation makes private memory defiant, Agard's alternating back-and-forth makes public reclamation visible on the page.

• Comparative topic sentence (AO1): While Rumens's passive verbs and compound adjectives register identity as a permanent mark the exile cannot lose, Agard's present-continuous verbs and carving-metaphor register identity as ongoing work the speaker actively builds.

Agard uses present-continuous verbs and a carving metaphor — "But now I checking out me own history / I carving out me identity" — turning identity into ongoing construction.

• Comparative close analysis (AO2): Rumens's passive "branded" registers the homeland's mark as a cattle-mark or criminal-mark, while "sunlight-clear" makes that mark glow rather than disfigure — political forces have made identity itself smuggled goods. Agard's "carving" registers identity-construction as ongoing work, and "Dem" registers the colonial education as a deliberate device for controlling the story.

• Comparative effects (AO2): Therefore Rumens's reader experiences courageous intimacy as the metaphor intensifies under pressure, while Agard's reader experiences liberation in real time as the present-continuous performs the reclamation it describes — a passive mark, set against active construction.

• Comparative author's purpose (AO1 + AO3): Rumens may have used the passive verbs to register identity as a mark exile cannot erase even as political forces make it smuggled goods. Agard can be read as using the carving present-continuous to insist that identity is made, not given — the speaker recovering authorship the curriculum had denied.

• Comparative context (AO3): Rumens wrote inside the Cold-War political exile of the early 1990s, when an insistent present tense was the only tense exiles could use without confirming that the homeland was lost. Agard wrote inside post-colonial Caribbean diaspora, where the suppressed figures — Toussaint L'Ouverture, Nanny de Maroon, Mary Seacole — were heroes the British curriculum had systematically left out.

• Synthesising hinge: Thus language itself carries whether identity is held privately or claimed publicly — Rumens's passive mark and smuggling verbs preserve identity inside the exile's body, Agard's present-continuous and carving verbs build identity at the public level the curriculum had refused.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Rumens and Agard both portray identity under institutional pressure, but their methods stand at opposite ends of the private-public scale — Rumens preserving identity through the exile's private record, Agard reclaiming it through public counter-naming.

• Controlling concept: Identity in the anthology's contemporary poems works as contested ground between institutional erasure and personal authorship — and the question of whether response is preservation or reclamation is the deepest move the cluster's post-colonial poems offer.

• Authors' purposes contrasted: Rumens may have written the lasting hold of belonging under political exile that turns homelands into memories. Agard can be read as writing the active work of reclamation against the institutional curriculum that erased Caribbean and African history — both poets refusing institutional authority over what belonging means.

• Ultimate moral about what comparing them reveals: Comparing the two reveals that the anthology's deepest move on identity is the recognition that institutional erasure can be answered through private memory or through public naming — and that whichever response the form takes, the work of identity is the work of refusing the institutions that tried to erase it.

Anchor poem (printed on the AQA paper):

Extract from, The Prelude — William Wordsworth

One summer evening (led by her) I found

A little boat tied to a willow tree

Within a rocky cove, its usual home.

Straight I unloosed her chain, and stepping in

Pushed from the shore. It was an act of stealth

And troubled pleasure, nor without the voice

Of mountain-echoes did my boat move on;

Leaving behind her still, on either side,

Small circles glittering idly in the moon,

Until they melted all into one track

Of sparkling light. But now, like one who rows,

Proud of his skill, to reach a chosen point

With an unswerving line, I fixed my view

Upon the summit of a craggy ridge,

The horizon's utmost boundary; far above

Was nothing but the stars and the grey sky.

She was an elfin pinnace; lustily

I dipped my oars into the silent lake,

And, as I rose upon the stroke, my boat

Went heaving through the water like a swan;

When, from behind that craggy steep till then

The horizon's bound, a huge peak, black and huge,

As if with voluntary power instinct,

Upreared its head. I struck and struck again,

And growing still in stature the grim shape

Towered up between me and the stars, and still,

For so it seemed, with purpose of its own

And measured motion like a living thing,

Strode after me. With trembling oars I turned,

And through the silent water stole my way

Back to the covert of the willow tree;

There in her mooring-place I left my bark, —

And through the meadows homeward went, in grave

And serious mood; but after I had seen

That spectacle, for many days, my brain

Worked with a dim and undetermined sense

Of unknown modes of being; o'er my thoughts

There hung a darkness, call it solitude

Or blank desertion. No familiar shapes

Remained, no pleasant images of trees,

Of sea or sky, no colours of green fields;

But huge and mighty forms, that do not live

Like living men, moved slowly through the mind

By day, and were a trouble to my dreams.

Question

Wordsworth's 1799-1850 blank-verse epic extract and Heaney's 1966 free-verse meditation both portray nature as a power that overwhelms human confidence, but their endings differ. • Three-phase sublime arc (Wordsworth) — confidence, terror, reflection.

• Free verse (Heaney) — a refusal of inherited convention.

• Military metaphor for weather (Heaney) — "strafes invisibly," "salvo," "bombarded".

• Oxymoron (Heaney) — two contradicting words joined together, e.g. "a huge nothing".

Heaney wrote inside a Northern Ireland whose landscape was as politically charged as the political weather audible in the title's anagram — opposite vocabularies for opposite positions.

• Synthesising hinge: Thus language itself carries whether nature can be made meaningful — Wordsworth's personification gives nature a moral consciousness that teaches, Heaney's emptied military oxymoron refuses every identifiable enemy the inherited Romantic vocabulary had supplied.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Wordsworth and Heaney both portray human confidence collapsing under nature, but the way their encounters end produces opposite positions. Wordsworth's terror turns into philosophical growth. Heaney's terror refuses to resolve.

• Controlling concept: Nature's power in this anthology depends on the historical moment of the witness — Wordsworth's Lake District pantheism turns nature into a moral teacher; Heaney's pre-Troubles Northern Ireland turns nature into political weather that refuses to identify itself.

• Authors' purposes contrasted: Wordsworth may have written the Romantic sublime as moral education. Heaney can be read as refusing that education because the political weather of 1966 had already made it impossible to defend — both poets registering nature's overwhelming power, but reaching opposite positions on whether the encounter teaches.

• Ultimate moral about what comparing them reveals: Comparing the two reveals that the cluster's deepest move on nature is the refusal of the Romantic frame — that overwhelming experience in the modern period cannot always be turned into philosophical growth, and that the form's responsibility is to register the refusal rather than supply the comfort.

Predicted anchor (AQA-named): Checking Out Me History by John Agard.

Suggested comparison poem (student choice — pick any other Power and Conflict poem): The Emigrée by Carol Rumens. Why: same pair-foundation as Q8 with the anchor reversed; the reclamation-versus-preservation differential in BP3 stages how institutional erasure can be answered through public counter-naming or private memory.

Alternative comparison candidates: London (Blake — institutional power written into the urban record, the same critique-from-below voice); Tissue (Dharker — paper and the materials that record or unrecord identity); Ozymandias (Shelley — the inscribed monument as institutional history that time itself unwrites).

Question

Compare how poets present the power of history in 'Checking Out Me History' and in one other poem from 'Power and conflict'. [30 marks]

Frame

Agard's 2005 Caribbean-creole counter-narrative and Rumens's 1993 free-verse meditation both portray history as institutional power over identity, but their responses differ. Agard reclaims through public counter-naming. Rumens preserves through private memory. The plan traces the difference between reclaiming and preserving. This Q reverses Q8's anchor — same poems, different anchor focus.

Pedagogical depth to thread. History in this anthology works as power exercised through what gets included in and left out of the official record. Both poems refuse institutional authority over the record, but their methods differ.

Technique gloss: As Q8 with anchor reversed; FORM / STRUCTURE / LANGUAGE BPs each still weave both poems.

• Hook (historical fact tied to thesis): Mary Seacole — the Jamaican-born nurse who funded her own passage to the Crimea in 1854 and treated British soldiers at her "British Hotel" near Balaclava — was systematically left out of the British school curriculum until the late twentieth century. Agard's 2005 poem corrects that erasure by raising her up among the suppressed figures the colonial curriculum had taught generations not to know.

• Building sentence (anthology context + comparison axis named): Rumens, writing twelve years earlier in 1993 from the Cold-War era when millions of homelands had become unreachable memories overnight, used a Mediterranean-suggesting unnamed city to register a different response to institutional erasure — the exile's private record of "sunlight-clear" memory. Both poets refuse institutional authority over the record, but their methods stand at opposite ends of the public-private scale.

• Thesis: Across form (Caribbean-creole alternating typography versus free verse with fairy-tale opening), structure (alternating back-and-forth between colonial fact and Caribbean counter-fact versus three-stanza escalation from threat to defiance), and language (active present-continuous and carving metaphor versus passive verbs of marking and the verb "smuggle"), Agard reclaims history through public counter-naming, while Rumens preserves it through private memory. Both poets together refuse the institutional authority that decides what history a person is allowed to inherit.

• Comparative topic sentence (AO1): While Agard uses Caribbean-creole alternating typography to reclaim history through public counter-naming, Rumens uses Standard-English free verse with a fairy-tale opening to preserve history through the exile's private record.

• Comparative technique + evidence (AO2 + AO1): Agard's Caribbean-creole free verse with alternating italic-and-roman typography opens on anaphora (the same opening phrase repeating) — "Dem tell me / Dem tell me / Wha dem want to tell me". Rumens's three eight-line stanzas open with the fairy-tale convention — "There once was a country... I left it as a child / but my memory of it is sunlight-clear".

• Comparative close analysis (AO2): Agard's creole "Dem" refuses Standard English as an inheritance of colonial authority, and the alternating typography stages the back-and-forth visually on the page. Rumens's fairy-tale opening softens the politics under which her violent verbs of marking do their work — a style that refuses to fit in, set against a style that fits in.

• Comparative effects (AO2): Thus Agard's reader witnesses liberation in real time as the typographic shifts enact the "checking out" the title names, while Rumens's reader experiences courageous intimacy going with the speaker into hostile territory — public reclamation, set against private preservation.

• Comparative author's purpose (AO1 + AO3): Agard can be read as choosing Caribbean creole because every community's words tie people to place — the creole itself part of the identity being reclaimed. Rumens may have chosen the unnamed city and fairy-tale opening to let the poem stand for any exile anywhere.

• Comparative context (AO3): Agard wrote in 2005 from post-colonial Caribbean diaspora, born in Guyana in 1949 and moved to Britain in 1977 inside the legacy of Macaulay's 1835 Minute on Education. Rumens wrote in 1993 from the Cold-War era when millions of homelands had become unreachable memories overnight.

• Synthesising hinge: Thus the choice of form carries whether response is public or private — Agard's creole alternating typography reclaims history outwardly, Rumens's Standard-English free verse preserves it inwardly.

• Comparative topic sentence (AO1): Where Agard structures the poem as an alternating back-and-forth between colonial fact and Caribbean counter-fact staged through typographic shifts, Rumens structures it as a three-stanza escalation from idealised memory through threat to defiant assertion.

• Comparative technique + evidence (AO2 + AO1): Agard's alternating refrain — "Dem tell me bout 1066 and all dat... But Toussaint L'Ouverture / no dem never tell me bout dat" — builds to the structural pivot "But now I checking out me own history / I carving out me identity". Rumens's escalation runs from "my memory of it is sunlight-clear" through "I have no passport, there's no way back" to "They mutter death, / and my shadow falls as evidence of sunlight".

• Comparative close analysis (AO2): Agard's monosyllable "But" performs the entire move from passive reception to active reclamation in one syllable, turning the speaker from an object of colonial story-telling into the maker of his own image. Rumens's pivot intensifies the sunlight metaphor as persecution mounts — "sunlight-clear" through "branded by an impression of sunlight" to "my shadow falls as evidence of sunlight".

• Comparative effects (AO2): Therefore Agard's reader witnesses liberation in real time as the typographic shifts enact the back-and-forth visibly, while Rumens's reader goes with the exile into hostile territory and finds the emotional landscape grows brighter under pressure — a visible back-and-forth, set against internal escalation.

• Comparative author's purpose (AO1 + AO3): Agard can be read as structuring the back-and-forth to make the colonial curriculum's selective inclusion visible on the page — the typography itself the political act. Rumens may have structured the escalation so the homeland's light intensifies as persecution mounts, making preservation defiant.

• Comparative context (AO3): Agard wrote inside the legacy of the colonial curriculum that taught generations to know English history while erasing Caribbean and African contributions. Rumens wrote inside the Cold-War-era political exile that had turned millions of homelands into memories overnight — opposite structural choices.

• Synthesising hinge: Thus structure determines whether history is reclaimed outwardly or preserved inwardly — Agard's alternating back-and-forth makes public reclamation visible on the page, Rumens's escalation makes private memory defiant.

• Comparative topic sentence (AO1): While Agard's present-continuous verbs and carving-metaphor register history as ongoing work the speaker actively builds, Rumens's passive verbs and compound adjectives register history as a permanent mark the exile cannot lose.

• Comparative technique + evidence (AO2 + AO1): Agard uses present-continuous verbs and a carving metaphor — "But now I checking out me own history / I carving out me identity" — and the accusing pronoun "Dem" — "Dem tell me bout Florence Nightingale and she lamp... but dem never tell me bout Mary Seacole". Rumens uses passive verbs of marking inside compound adjectives — "now I am branded by an impression of sunlight" — and the closing "smuggle it across the border".

• Comparative close analysis (AO2): Agard's "carving" registers history-construction as ongoing work, and "Dem" registers the colonial education as a deliberate device for controlling the story. Rumens's passive "branded" registers the homeland's mark as a cattle-mark or criminal-mark, while "sunlight-clear" makes that mark glow rather than disfigure.

• Comparative effects (AO2): Therefore Agard's reader experiences liberation in real time as the present-continuous performs the reclamation it describes, while Rumens's reader experiences courageous intimacy as the metaphor intensifies under pressure — active construction, set against a passive mark.

• Comparative author's purpose (AO1 + AO3): Agard can be read as using the carving present-continuous to insist that history is made, not given — the speaker recovering authorship the curriculum had denied. Rumens may have used the passive verbs to register history as a mark exile cannot erase, even as political forces make identity smuggled goods.

• Comparative context (AO3): Agard wrote inside post-colonial Caribbean diaspora, where the suppressed figures — Toussaint L'Ouverture, Nanny de Maroon, Mary Seacole — were heroes the British curriculum had systematically left out. Rumens wrote inside the Cold-War political exile of the early 1990s, when an insistent present tense was the only tense exiles could use without confirming that the homeland was lost.

• Synthesising hinge: Thus language itself carries whether history is claimed publicly or held privately — Agard's present-continuous and carving verbs build history at the public level the curriculum had refused, Rumens's passive mark and smuggling verbs preserve history inside the exile's body.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Agard and Rumens both portray history as institutional power over identity, but their responses stand at opposite ends of the public-private scale — Agard reclaiming through public counter-naming, Rumens preserving through private memory.

• Controlling concept: History in the anthology's contemporary poems works as power exercised through what gets included in and left out of the official record — and the question of whether response is reclamation or preservation is the deepest move the cluster's post-colonial poems offer.

• Authors' purposes contrasted: Agard can be read as writing the post-colonial counter-narrative that the curriculum had suppressed. Rumens may have written the exile's private record that political pressure had made smuggled goods — both poets refusing institutional authority over the record.

• Ultimate moral about what comparing them reveals: Comparing the two reveals that the anthology's deepest move on history is the recognition that what counts as history is itself a form of power — and that whether the response is public reclamation or private preservation, the work of identity is the work of refusing the institutions that decided what history a person was allowed to inherit.

Two critical voices to deploy

Sophicly deploys two named critical voices across the P&C corpus. Use them sparingly — once per essay maximum — and only when they sharpen the close analysis.

• William Dowling (Rutgers) on My Last Duchess — attribute as "Rutgers critic" or "Rutgers lecturer". The reading: the Duke is a "monster of pride", "untouchable because he's got wealth, power and ancestry"; "I choose / Never to stoop" are "the most menacing words in English poetry" because they encode willingness to kill rather than yield. Use in Q2, Q3 (Browning side).

• Helen Vendler on form-as-meaning. The reading: form is the first argument a poem makes; when the form fits the content, the poem reinforces its argument; when the form fights the content (Shelley's broken sonnet about a broken tyrant; Heaney's free verse about a community that has no formal shelter), the contradiction IS the argument. Use across any Q where formal differential is the analytical event — particularly Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4, Q9.

(Attribute readings to these named voices; do not name Mr Bruff in essays. Bruff is a teaching guide for revision, not an academic source for citation.)

Final reminder bullet

• Name WHICH power. Power and Conflict covers many kinds of power; every comparison thesis must specify which power the two poems examine.

• Trace the EFFECT difference. Comparison reveals what parallel description cannot: the methods take the same theme, and the effects on the reader are the analytical event.

• One anchor quote per BP — a single line that carries the topic-sentence concept.

• Discourse markers when switching poets — Thus / Whereas / By contrast / However / Indeed / In conclusion.

• Hedged author's-purpose claims — may have / can be read as / a defensible reading is.

• No "shows" as an analytical verb — illustrates / portrays / registers / enacts / dramatises / exposes / reveals.

• No arrows — through / from / leading to / which produces.

• No feminist-critique framework — reframe gender as honour code / dynastic marriage / property law / social order.

• Hook must tie thesis — a fact, quote, question, or metaphor that captures THIS essay's specific argument.

• Sentence cap ~35-45 words / ~3 lines. Split rather than shorten.
